original_reasoning: Arabic purity/plain explicitness plausibly aligns with Greek 'made wholly of silk' as a refined unmixed quality.
Greek is carrying bath-water, while the Arabic gloss names a well; the direct water-source to water-carrying path is plausible.
ʿaḍara includes reporting or blurting out a word; Greek loídora is abusive speech, a plausible specialization from utterance into verbal attack.
Arabic boastful confrontation can drift into verbal fighting, so the quarrel sense is plausible though not direct.
Greek means repeated calling, and the Arabic gloss includes addressing or talking to a woman; both preserve an active speech-approach core.
Dust conceptually spreads into many parts, giving a plausible abstract bridge.
Arabic أقذره points to defilement or foulness, which plausibly drifts to ill-omened or profaning speech.
Arabic root points to binding and fastening, Greek λυ- carries loosening/releasing; the pair preserves both sides of the same conceptual axis.
